pages.text: Pullout behavior of inclined steel ﬁber in an ultra-high strength cementitious matrix Yun Lee a, Su-Tae Kang b,*, Jin-Keun Kim c a Department of Architectural Engineering, College of Engineering, Ewha Womans University, 11-1 Daehyun-dong, Seodaemun-gu, Seoul 120-750, South Korea b Structural Engineering & Bridges Research Division, Korea Institute of Construction Technology, 1190 Simindae-ro, Ilsanseo-gu, Goyang 411-712, South Korea c Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), 373-1 Guseong-dong, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-701, South Korea article info Article history: Received 10 July 2009 Received in revised form 12 January 2010 Accepted 25 March 2010 Available online 18 April 2010 Keywords: Pullout Steel ﬁber Ultra-high strength Inclined ﬁber Bond strength abstract In this study, as a part of research to characterize the tensile properties of steel ﬁber reinforced ultra-high strength cementitious composites, pullout tests of steel ﬁber were performed to evaluate the effect of ﬁber inclination angle on the load direction and an analytical pullout model was derived considering this effect. The ﬁber inclination angles considered in the pullout tests were 0 /C176,1 5/C176,3 0/C176,4 5/C176, and 60 /C176. From the pullout tests, it was observed that the largest peak load was obtained at an angle of 30 /C176or 45 /C176, and the peak slip increased as the ﬁbers were oriented at a more inclined angle. Based on the experimental results, an analytical pullout behavior model considering ﬁber inclination was proposed. In order to take into account the effect of ﬁber inclination in the pullout model, apparent shear strengths ( s(app)) and slip coefﬁcient ( b) were introduced to express the variation of pullout peak load and the augmentation of peak slip as the inclined angle increases. These variables are expressed as functions of the inclined angle (/). /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Brittle materials such as mortars and ceramics exhibit low toughness and poor resistance to cracking. However, the incorpo- ration of ﬁbers can delay the occurrence of cracks in concrete and restrain their propagation, thus endowing substantially higher energy absorption capability and toughness compared to plain concrete. This beneﬁcial effect can be attributed to ﬁber bridging at cracks by the pullout process of the ﬁbers, the main mechanism of ﬁber reinforcement. Fiber reinforced concrete (FRC) resists tensile forces through a composite action of the matrix and the ﬁbers. A part of the tensile force is resisted by the matrix, while the other part is resisted by the ﬁbers. Each of these resistances is determined by the stress transfer at the ﬁber–matrix interface, which is achieved by the bond deﬁned as the shear stress acting at the interface. Before any cracking has taken place, elastic stress transfer is dominant. At more advanced stages of loading, debonding across the interface usually takes place, and frictional slip governs stress transfer at the interface. Therefore, the mechanical properties of FRC, especially its tensile strength, tensile stress–strain curve, and toughness, are sensitively inﬂuenced by the bond characteristics at the ﬁber–matrix interface [1–4]. Accordingly, it is necessary to study the bond properties between the matrix and ﬁber prior to examin- ing the various mechanical properties of FRC. Ultra-high strength cementitious matrix is composed o

the slip displacement due to friction, and therefore can be neglected. After analyzing the pullout behavior in the case of aligned ﬁber based on the model given by Naaman et al., the effect of ﬁber incli- nation angle on the pullout curve was quantitatively estimated and this effect was reﬂected in the modiﬁed pullout model. 4.1. Pullout behavior of aligned steel ﬁber 4.1.1. Ascending behavior As mentioned earlier, the ascending region in the pullout load– slip curve can be identiﬁed by two regions corresponding to the perfectly bonded state and partially debonded state. In the per- fectly bonded state, the bond force–slip behavior is deﬁned by Eq. (7), where Q can be obtained by means of the physical proper- ties of the matrix and ﬁbers. After deciding Q, k can be determined using the value of the initial linear slope of the experimental load– slip curve. In the partially debonded region, the values of smax and sf in Eqs. (9) and (10) should be determined. The peak load, Pmax, normally occurs in the partially debonded region. In general, if sf is smaller than smax, the pullout load after reach- ing the peak load drops suddenly to a level corresponding to the frictional slip resistance generated when the ﬁber is fully debond- ed [13]. Fig. 9 shows the experimental results obtained in this study; note that a sudden load drop did not appear. Therefore, smax and sf can be assumed to be identical for the modeling of the ﬁber pullout behavior in the ultra-high strength cementitious matrix, and the peak load, P max, is obtained when the ﬁber is fully debond- ed along the embedded length. Consequently, the values of smax and sf can be calculated using only Eq. (9), without requiring the equation related to Dmax, which is likely to include slight experi- mental errors in the measured values. The obtained values of smax and sf are both 6.80 MPa, and when Pmax = 27.77 kN, the slip Dmax is calculated to be 0.0148 mm. Fig. 10 compares the pullout load– slip curve obtained by means of the model with the experimental results. 4.1.2. Descending behavior Eqs. (11) and (12) are established based on the following equa- tion considering a Poisson’s effect after full debonding and expo- nential decay of the frictional bond shear stress ( sf). P ¼ 1 /C0 exp /C0 4mf lx Ef df 1þmm Em þ 1/C0 mf Ef /C16/C17 8 < : 9 = ; 2 4 3 5 dEf pdf 2mf ð13Þ where x is the embedded length of the ﬁber with x = l /C0 D + Do and is valid for D P Do. In this relation, it should be noted that while x varies from l to zero, D changes between Do and l + Do. However, if we consider that there is a small pullout load when the ﬁber is al- most pulled out, the slip at that time should be equal to the original embedded ﬁber length as the elastic deformation of the ﬁber is recovered. Therefore, it is reasonable that D varies from Do to l while x varies from l to zero. The embedded length, x, is thus ex- pressed as x ¼ l l /C0 Do ðl /C0 DÞð 14Þ d is the relative displacement between the ﬁber and matrix due to radial shrinkage of the matrix. This variable decreases due to abra- sion of the matrix around the ﬁber according to the progress of fric- tional slip behavior. Considering this phenomenon, Naaman et al. [19] expressed d as the following through a comparison with exper- imental results. d ¼ do e/C0ð D/C0 Do Þg/C0 1e/C0ðl Þg 1 /C0 1e/C0ð l/C0 DþDo Þg ð15Þ where do can be obtained by substituting x = l and applying P = pdfsf in Eq. (13). This study analyzed the experimental results usin

embedded length and surface treatment of ﬁber to improve the bond–slip properties. The inclination angle of a ﬁber in a cementitious matrix has a strong inﬂuence on the pullout resistance. Although several researchers have performed experiments to investigate the effect of ﬁber inclination angle, the focus was mostly on the peak pullout load, and the effect is still disputable [3,12–14]. However, it is gen- erally agreed that the effect of ﬁber inclination angle on the pullout load and pullout energy is totally dependent on the ﬁber aspect ra- tio, the ﬁber shape (straight, hooked, corrugated etc.), and material properties such as yield strength, and whether the ﬁber material is metallic or synthetic. As a preliminary research to ﬁnd out the tensile behavior of steel ﬁber reinforced ultra-high strength cementitious composites, the analytical pullout model which can consider the effect of various ﬁ- ber orientations on the pullout behaviors is suggested in this study. The pullout test results were then exploited to derive pullout mod- els by changing the inclined angle of steel ﬁber in each specimen. Most analytical pullout models proposed to date deal with the behavior of aligned ﬁber pulled out from the matrix [15–22]. Although some researchers have proposed models to predict the pullout behavior of inclined ﬁber [23,24], the approaches required a numerical procedure and were not given by analytical formulae. Therefore, they are not appropriate for estimation of the tensile properties based on both pullout behaviors of randomly oriented ﬁbers and the probability distribution of ﬁber orientation. This led us to propose an analytical pullout model that is readily appli- cable for this purpose. 2. Analytical pullout model for aligned ﬁber 2.1. Overview of theoretical pullout models Numerous researchers have performed analytical studies to clarify the bond mechanism of the ﬁbers in a cementitous matrix. Wang et al. [15] proposed an analytic model for the prediction of the pullout behavior, which is deﬁned by the curve of the pullout load versus end slip displacement (or crack width), for synthetic ﬁ- ber in concrete. This model does not consider the effect of the Pois- son’s ratio, and the authors note that, in the case of ﬁbers with a low elastic modulus, the model assumes that the bond behavior is governed by the frictional bond shear strength with the elastic bond shear strength having practically no effect. This model is thus appropriate for synthetic ﬁbers exhibiting low elastic moduli such as nylon or polypropylene. Thereafter, Stang et al. [16] and Lin et al. [17], among others, continued to develop analytical pullout models based on these assumptions. Lin et al. [17] suggested a theoretical pullout behavior model for straight ﬁbers by applying a simple stress analysis and energy balance principle under the assumptions of Wang et al. [15]. This model adopted two material parameters, the critical energy release rate (alternatively called the chemical bond strength) and the frictional bond strength, and considered slip hardening after debonding. With respect to steel ﬁbers, Nammur and Naaman [18] pro- posed an analytical model of the bond at the interface between steel ﬁbers and cementitious composites, assuming an idealized bond–slip relationship. The assumed relationship is bilinear and elastic-perfectly frictional. This model was limited to bond stress in the interface and does not deal with the pullout behavior. Nam- man et al. [1

investigated in the present study, difﬁculties in gripping the free end of the ﬁber are inevitably encountered. Accordingly, this study adopts the double-sided pullout test using multiple ﬁbers so as to overcome the aforementioned problems, and examines the pullout behavior using the average value relative to one ﬁber. 3.1. Experimental design The mix proportion adopted in this study is summarized in Ta- ble 1 and is applied identically to all the tested matrixes. The ﬁbers used in the test are steel ﬁbers with a tensile strength of 2500 MPa, a diameter of 0.2 mm, and a length of 13 mm. A total of 32 steel ﬁ- bers are embedded in each specimen at an inclination angle with respect to the pullout direction of 0 /C176,1 5/C176,3 0 /C176,4 5/C176, and 60/C176. The pullout test specimens were fabricated as double-sided pullout specimens using multiple ﬁbers. Fig. 3 depicts the manu- facturing process of the specimens. The process can be brieﬂy de- scribed as follows. First, a PE sheet is adhered to a steel plate (Fig. 4 ) to ensure sealing, and the ﬁbers are placed into the plate at a depth equivalent to half of their length. The plate has holes that enable the location and inclination angle to be set accurately. The plate is then positioned at the center of the PVC mold and di- vided into two portions before casting mortar on one face. After 24 h of wet curing at ambient temperature, the central steel plate is removed. At this time, the PE sheet and the steel ﬁbers are bonded to the matrix and retain their shapes. Thereafter, the ma- trix is casted on the other side and subjected identically to 24 h of wet curing at ambient temperature. Finally, after the mold is re- moved, the specimen is subjected to steam curing at 90 /C176C during 48 h. Fig. 5 illustrates the conﬁguration and dimensions of the specimen fabricated through this process. Tests were performed through displacement control using a universal testing machine with a capacity of 10 kN, as shown in Fig. 6 . The load–slip relationships were obtained from the experi- ments. The slip was measured by means of a clip gauge positioned at the center of the specimen. 3.2. Experimental results Test results on pullout tests for multiple ﬁbers are reported in Table 2, where peak slip is the slip corresponding to the peak load and the unit peak load refers to the value obtained by dividing the peak load by the number of ﬁbers and the pullout work is deﬁned as the area under the pullout load versus slip curve. Pullout behaviors corresponding to each orientation of the ﬁ- bers to the pullout direction, that is, 0 /C176,1 5/C176,3 0/C176,4 5/C176, and 60/C176, are plotted in Fig. 7 . From the pullout response obtained for the incli- nation angle, it was clear that the ﬁber orientation with respect to the crack plane has a deﬁnite inﬂuence on the response. It could also be observed that the largest maximum pullout loads and pull- out work occurred at orientation angles of 30 /C176or 45 /C176(Fig. 8 ) and that increased ﬁber inclination angle accelerated peak slip. In addi- tion, the initial ascending slope of the load–slip curve appears to be smaller with a larger ﬁber inclination angle. The obtained results including peak pullout load and slip and the ascending and descending shape of the pullout curve can be used to derive a modiﬁed pullout model that takes into account the effect of ﬁber inclination angle. The derived model is based on the pullout model of Naaman et al. [19], which is applicab

The local bond shear stress ( s) versus local slip (S ) relationship in the elastic region (fully bonded) of Fig. 1 can be related through the bond modulus j as follows. s ¼ jS ð2Þ where j is assumed to be constant, and the slip S is deﬁned as follows. The unknowns A and B can be obtained using the two boundary conditions, F(0) = 0 and F(l)= P. The critical load Pcrit stands for the load when the shear stress acting on the ﬁber and matrix at x = l reaches its maximum value, smax. The critical load can be calculated by substituting s(x = l)= smax in Eq. (5). Pcrit ¼ pdf smax k 1 /C0 e/C0 2kl 1 /C0 1 Q /C16/C17 1 þ e/C0 2klðÞ þ 1 Q /C16/C17 2e/C0 kl 2 4 3 5 ð6Þ When a force P 6 Pcrit, the ﬁbers remain perfectly bonded to the matrix and the interface is kept in an elastic bond condition. The pullout behavior of the ﬁber can be divided into three dis- tinct regions. The ﬁrst region is the elastic range, where the ﬁber and matrix are perfectly bonded. The second region is the partial debonding region, during which a part of the ﬁber is bonded while the other part remains perfectly bonded. The third region is the fully debonded region, where the pullout behavior is governed by only frictional bond shear stress at the interface. 2.2.1. Perfectly bonded region When P 6 Pcrit, the ﬁber preserves a perfect bond with the ma- trix. In this case, the pullout load–slip behavior is linear and can be deﬁned as follows. P D /C18/C19 ¼ kAmEm Q /C0 2 1 þ e/C0 kl 1 /C0 e/C0 kl ð7Þ 2.2.2. Partial debonding region When P>P crit, a part of the ﬁber is debonded from the matrix while the remaining part is still fully bonded to the matrix. Here, the pullout resistant load (P ) is the result of the resistance due to the ﬁber in the bonded zone (P b) and the resistance due to the ﬁber in the debonded zone (P d). P ¼ Pb þ Pd ð8Þ Let u be the length of the debonded zone. Assuming that the bond force acting on this length is provided by the friction with a con- stant value of sf, the force acting on length u (Pd) can be calculated. Furthermore, Pb can be computed by replacing l by l–u in Eq. (6). Accordingly, the pullout load and the corresponding slip displace- ment in the partially debonded state are given by P ¼ pdf sf u þ pdf smax k 1 /C0 e/C0 2kðl/C0 uÞ 2 Q /C16/C17 e/C0 kðl/C0 uÞ þ 1 /C0 1 Q /C16/C17 f1 þ e/C0 2kðl/C0 uÞg ð9Þ D ¼ PðQ /C0 1Þu /C0 pdf sf u2 2 ðQ /C0 2Þþð P /C0 pdf sf uÞ 1/C0 e/C0 kðl/C0 uÞ 1þe/C0 kðl/C0 uÞ /C16/C17 Q /C0 2 k /C0 pdf sf ul no AmEm ð10Þ 2.2.3. Fully debonded region Once complete debonding has occurred at the ﬁber–matrix interface, the ﬁber acts as a rigid body.

tensile strength of 2990 MPa. Accordingly, based on the obtained experimental results, this study suggests a new frictional slip behavior model applicable to ultra-high strength mortar. The pro- posed model is expressed in Eq. (16). d ¼ do exp /C0 gðD /C0 DoÞa/C8/C9 ð16Þ where a is a constant determining the initial slope of the frictional slip behavior; and g is a constant related to the shape of the expo- nentially descending branch in the bond–slip curve. Once g is deter- mined, a can be obtained by comparing the initial slope of the descending branch, which is obtained by substituting D = Do after differentiating Eq. (13) with respect to D, with experimental results. When g = 0.05, an overall decreasing trend of the experimental re- sults was satisfactorily simulated. Fig. 13 compares the behavior ob- tained by the model with the experimental results. 4.2. Pullout behavior of inclined steel ﬁber 4.2.1. Snubbing and matrix spalling effect In the case where the ﬁbers are not positioned in the tensile load direction but instead are inclined, the bridging force will be increased [14,28,29]. This phenomenon is called the ‘snubbing ef- fect’. The corresponding crack opening displacement also increases due to the snubbing effect. In addition, ﬁber with an angle inclined toward the pullout load direction causes a stress concentration at the ﬁber exit and conse- quent local failure of the supporting matrix [30,31]. The length of matrix spalling increases with a larger inclined angle, which leads to a decrease of pullout load and an increase of the corresponding crack opening displacement. Matrix spalling at the ﬁber exit can compromise the effectiveness of ﬁber reinforcement. For the bond behavior of inclined steel ﬁbers, the gradual spalling of the matrix should also be considered together with the snubbing effect. The combined effects of the snubbing and matrix spalling were considered when modeling the bond behavior of the inclined ﬁbers. 4.2.2. Ascending behavior Modeling was implemented through a comparison of the pull- out test results according to the change of the inclination of the ﬁ- bers. Modeling for the bond behavior of inclined ﬁber was based on the pullout model for aligned ﬁber. The modeling of the bond behavior for inclined ﬁbers considered the variation of load due to the snubbing effect and matrix spalling effect based on the mod- el when the ﬁber inclined angle (/ ) is equal to zero. This was accomplished by introducing the apparent bond strength ( smax(app), sf(app)), which is described as a function of the inclined angle /.I n addition, the increase of the slip displacement was reﬂected by multiplying the slip displacement corresponding to / = 0 by the coefﬁcient b, which is also a function of the inclined angle /. Table 3 gives the values of smax(app), sf(app),a n db obtained through comparison with the experimental results for each ﬁber inclination angle, as well as the corresponding P max. In this study, smax(app) is equal to sf(app) for each ﬁber inclination angle, since smax and sf were the same for the aligned ﬁber. The Levenberg–Marquardt algorithm was selected for the nonlinear regression method to ﬁt Fig. 12. The effect of g on the shape of descending branch in pullout behavior (f = 0). Fig. 13. The parameter b was determined by calculating Dpeak(u)/Dpeak(0) ratio obtained from the experiments, where Dpeak(u) denotes the peak slip dis- placement corresponding to the peak load with / and Dpeak(0) is the peak slip displacement corresponding to the peak load for the aligned ﬁber. Fig. 14 compares the experimentally obtained load–slip curves and the predicted curves from the model using smax(app), sf(app) and b with different ﬁber inclination angles. This study attempts to express smax(app), sf(app) and b as func- tions of / based on the results of Table 3. To express these quanti- ties as functions of / considering the snubbing effect and matrix spalling effect for Pmax, the snubbing effect could be considered by the following equation [14,30]. Pð/Þ¼ ef /Pð/ ¼ 0Þð 18Þ where f is the snubbing friction coefﬁcient. While the value of f var- ies according to the type of ﬁber and the strength of the matrix, its value ranges between 0.5 and 1 for nylon, polypropylene, PVA or steel ﬁber in mortar with normal strength. The matrix spalling effect can be considered by the following equation, assuming that a load reduction does not occur when / = 0 and that the pullout force does not act on the ﬁber when / = p/2.
